title: Ukraine stopped waiting for the war to end
gist: Cory Alpert argues that after 12 years of war, Ukrainians have stopped imagining a defined 'postwar' moment and are instead planning politics, security guarantees, and personal life around permanent insecurity — a shift that hardens Ukraine's rejection of territory-for-peace deals and demands Western security guarantees credible enough to reshape Russian invasion calculus, not reassurance that peace is near.

insights.claim: Foreign governments discuss conflict using a grammar of defined periods — ceasefires, reconstruction, elections, peacekeeping — that follow a definitive shift from 'war' to 'postwar,' but in Ukraine, after 12 years of fighting Russia (a third of its independent existence), the idea of a peaceful 'postwar' moment has become almost impossible to imagine.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: Reframes how outside actors should model Ukraine — not as a country waiting for a phase transition to peace, but one restructuring itself around durable insecurity, with direct implications for how reconstruction and security-guarantee planning should be sequenced.
insights.locator: After 12 years – a third of Ukraine's existence

insights.claim: Away from the front lines, daily life in much of Ukraine looks mostly normal on days without a major Russian strike, but the war has stopped feeling like an emergency with an endpoint and has instead become the atmosphere in which politics now happens, with an absence of the future tense in political discussion.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: Explains why normal political planning (successors, elections, reconstruction timelines) is stalled even amid apparent day-to-day normalcy — a useful corrective for outside observers reading calm streets as a sign of political stability.
insights.locator: The war has stopped feeling like an emergency

insights.claim: A Ukrainian journalist who regularly meets Zelenskyy and senior politicians laughed when asked who is in the running to be the next president, calling the question pointless.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: A concrete, sourced data point showing succession planning is effectively frozen inside Ukraine's political elite, complicating any near-term election timeline.
insights.locator: I sat with a Ukrainian journalist

themes.theme: Why compromise is off the table

insights.claim: Nearly half of Ukrainians do not expect the war to end before 2027, and large majorities reject a peace deal that trades territory or future military capacity for a settlement they feel merely freezes the conflict in place rather than delivering real security.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: Quantifies domestic political constraints on any negotiated settlement — a territory-for-peace deal faces a hard public-opinion ceiling in Ukraine regardless of Western pressure.
insights.locator: Nearly half of Ukrainians do not expect

insights.claim: Older Ukrainians describe the 2014 Russian annexation of Crimea as a failure of inheritance, carrying a sense of shame that acquiescence then did not spare Ukraine a larger war but instead made the next one easier for Russia to imagine — a memory now driving resistance to any deal seen as repeating that pattern.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: Explains the psychological mechanism behind Ukraine's hard line on territorial concessions — 2014 is treated as proof that concessions invite escalation, not peace.
insights.quote: There is a sense of shame that acquiescence in 2014 did not spare Ukraine a larger war – it just made the next one easier for Russia to imagine.
insights.locator: There is a sense of shame that acquiescence

insights.claim: The 1994 Budapest Memorandum, under which Ukraine gave up the world's third-largest nuclear arsenal (behind only Russia and the US) in exchange for an independence guarantee, has become a live topic of informal political discussion, reflecting a collapse in Ukrainian faith in security guarantees offered by either the West or Russia — though there is no serious proposal for Ukraine to rebuild nuclear weapons now.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: Signals eroding Ukrainian trust in paper security guarantees, raising the bar for what kind of Western commitment (beyond declaratory assurances) would actually be credible to Kyiv.
insights.locator: The Budapest Memorandum has now become a live topic

insights.claim: There is no appetite for elections in Ukraine right now, given that a fifth of the country is under Russian occupation and any election would almost certainly be attacked and meddled with by Russia; a journalist's framing captures the stakes — if Ukrainian tanks rolled into Red Square, Zelenskyy would stay in power as long as he liked, but if the war ends in 'a long sigh,' the political aperture becomes much wider.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: Sets out the specific conditions under which Ukrainian elections could plausibly happen versus remain indefinitely deferred — a key variable for political-risk assessments of Ukraine.
insights.locator: As that journalist put it to me

insights.claim: Ukrainian politics have rarely had stable parties with their own independent identity; instead, parties tend to spring up as vehicles for individuals running for president, who then seek parliamentary support based on their own position.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: A structural feature of Ukrainian politics that will shape any post-election landscape — expect personality-driven coalitions rather than durable party platforms.
insights.locator: Politics in Ukraine have rarely had stable parties

themes.theme: What the West should offer instead

insights.claim: Ukraine's drone economy offers a path to defence-industrial integration and economic resilience while the war continues, and the author argues the West should focus on security guarantees credible enough to reshape Russian calculations about a future invasion, rather than trying to reassure Ukrainians there will not be another one.
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: A specific, actionable policy recommendation: Western aid strategy should prioritise deterrence-credible guarantees and drone-industrial cooperation over reconstruction-phase thinking.
insights.locator: The West should focus on security guarantees

insights.claim: The author closes with a historical analogy: Europeans living through what is now called the Hundred Years War did not realise they were in one continuous conflict, and Ukraine understands it cannot end this war without a guarantee that 'another 88 years of war will not follow.'
insights.speaker: Cory Alpert
insights.so_what: Frames the core strategic risk for Western planners: without a credible long-term security guarantee, any pause could simply be an interlude rather than an actual end to the conflict.
insights.quote: Ukraine understands that it cannot end this war without a guarantee that another 88 years of war will not follow.
insights.locator: Europeans at the time did not realise
